title: Chapter 12: The Glitch
content: The elevator smells like the sea.

I'm alone in the glass box, rising through Sterling Capital's steel skeleton, thirty-four floors of Manhattan verticality, and the air hits me like a cliff edge — salt and rain and wet stone, so vivid I can taste it on my tongue, brine and minerals and something green, something alive. My throat closes. My eyes water.
I'm in the middle of Midtown. The closest ocean is forty miles away.
I look down at my hands.
Blood.

For one flickering millisecond — the span of a single stuttering heartbeat — my fingers are slick with it. Dark and warm and spreading across my palms, between my knuckles, under my fingernails. Julian C's blood, from the night I stitched his eyebrow, the thread pulling through torn skin, his breath shallow and steady against my wrist.
I gasp. The sound bounces off the glass walls.
I look again.

Gone. Clean hands. Pale skin. Short nails with chipped nude polish. The faint ink stain on my index finger from a leaking pen yesterday. My hands, ordinary hands, gripping the brass handrail like it's the only solid thing left in the world.
The elevator dings.

Thirty-fourth floor. The doors slide open to polished concrete and recessed lighting and the particular hush of a floor where money moves quietly. I step out on shaking legs and pray nobody's watching the elevator bank at 8:47 on a Tuesday morning.
Nobody is. It's fine. I'm fine.
I'm not fine.

The conference room fills with the usual suspects — Marcus from M&A, Priya from Risk, three junior analysts whose names I should know but can't access through the fog in my brain. I take my seat at the far end of the table, notebook open, pen uncapped, performing the choreography of someone who arrived prepared.

Julian stands at the head of the room, sleeves rolled to his forearms, pointer clicking through slides. Quarterly analysis. European market exposure. Rebalancing recommendations. His voice is steady and precise, a metronome counting time in projections and basis points.
I'm following along. I am. I'm tracking the data, parsing the strategy, doing my job — until the Irish sea materializes on the conference table.

It's like a hologram overlaid on reality, translucent but undeniable. Grey water churning under a bruised sky. Rain streaming down glass — the window at Paws & Pause, Julian C's shelter, his sanctuary, that tiny café where the storm pressed against the windows and he pressed his forehead to mine and said *stay*. I can see the white-capped waves through the table's polished surface, smell the salt again, hear the rattle of rain on a tin roof that exists six hundred miles and one timeline away.
I flinch.
Not a subtle shift-in-my-seat flinch. A full-body jerk, shoulders snapping back, pen clattering against the table. Marcus glances over. Priya looks up from her laptop.
The sea vanishes. The conference table is just a conference table — mahogany veneer, cable ports, someone's abandoned water bottle.
But Julian saw.

His eyes track to my face and hold there, steady as a scope. He doesn't stop talking — "—recommending we reduce exposure by fifteen percent and reallocate to —" — but he's not looking at the screen anymore. He's not looking at the room. He's watching me, the smallest crease between his brows, and he doesn't look away for the remaining eleven minutes of his presentation.
I don't breathe for the remaining eleven minutes of his presentation.

The moment the meeting ends, I move. Phone, notebook, pen — swept into my bag. Out the door, past the cluster of analysts debating lunch options, into the hallway, left at the water cooler, through the door marked with the stick figure in the triangle.
Bathroom. Stall. Lock engaged.
I sit on the closed toilet lid and open the Fork app with hands that won't stop trembling.
The black interface loads. The red text blooms.
**INTEGRITY: 71%**
**TEMPORAL ECHOES: ACTIVE**
**NEW ALERT: Recommended: Reduce shift frequency. Continued high-frequency shifting may cause irreversible timeline convergence.**
Irreversible.

I've never seen that word before. The app has always been pragmatic — *use caution*, *monitor symptoms*, *recommended rest period*. Clinical, measured, helpful. The app doesn't do catastrophic. The app doesn't do irreversible.
I touch the black icon. The white text cursor blinks.
**What does timeline convergence mean?**
The response appears instantly, as it always does, flat and unnerving in its calm:
**When boundaries between timelines dissolve, events, objects, and memories may cross between worlds. This process accelerates with use. It cannot be reversed.**
Cannot. Be. Reversed.
I read it again. And again. The words don't change. The letters sit there in their sterile white type, indifferent to the fact that they just cracked my chest open and rearranged everything inside.

Boundaries between timelines dissolving. Events crossing. Objects crossing. Memories crossing. That's why I smelled the sea in a Manhattan elevator. That's why I saw the Irish coast on a conference table. That's why there was blood on my hands — not my blood, not this timeline's blood, but real blood from a real wound in a real café six hundred miles away.
My hands. My body. My one body, being lived in by three different women.
I close my eyes. Press my forehead against the cool metal of the stall divider. The bathroom's fluorescent hum fills the silence.
The app buzzes again. I don't look. I already know the number is lower.
I stay in the stall for four more minutes. Then I fix my face in the mirror, smooth down my blazer, and walk back to my desk like a person who has her shit together.
I do not have my shit together.

The glitches do not stop. They do not slow down. They come in waves, overlapping, relentless, as if the boundaries the app warned me about have already started to dissolve and every moment of every life I've lived is fighting for space in my senses at the same time.

I'm reading Julian A's email — *Re: Q3 positioning, restructure the emerging market thesis* — and I hear Julian C's voice, clear as a bell, low and rough: *You stayed.* Not on the page. Not from a speaker. From inside my own skull, spoken in a tone that doesn't exist in this timeline, not from this man, not in this world.

I take a sip of office coffee — burnt, bitter, lukewarm, the same breakroom swill I've been drinking for two years — and my mouth floods with the taste of Sancerre. Crisp and mineral and cold, with that particular citrus note, the wine from the restaurant in Timeline B where Julian B ordered without looking at the menu and I laughed because who *does* that and he looked at me like I'd hung the moon.

I set the cup down. My right hand tingles, the palm specifically, the exact center where Julian B pressed his thumb during that walk along the Seine, his hand light and warm and questioning — not holding mine, just *touching*, just testing the voltage between two points.
Three lives. Bleeding into one body.
I am a house with the walls coming down.

I type an answer to Julian A's email. Delete it. Type it again. Delete it again. My fingers are someone else's fingers. My coffee is someone else's wine. My memory is a radio scanning through frequencies, landing on fragments — his voice, his hand, his blood — and I can't find the dial to make it stop.
"Come with me."
Julian A's voice. Julian A's shadow falling across my desk at 3:14 in the afternoon. Not a request. Not even close to a request.

I look up. His face gives me nothing — that particular Vance-brand blankness that could mean *I need you for a task* or *you're about to be fired* or *I'm about to say something I'll regret*. I can't read him. I've never been able to read this version of him. He's the first Julian, the original, the one I have the least intimacy with, and right now I cannot tell if he's about to save me or end me.
I follow him anyway.

He takes me to an empty office — one of the interims, the glass-walled boxes that Sterling uses for visiting partners. The door clicks shut behind us. The sound is small and final, a bone settling into its socket.
"Something is wrong with you."

Not cruel. Clinical. The way a doctor delivers a diagnosis to a patient he's been observing for weeks — with the detachment of professional distance, but also with the concern of someone who's been *watching*, who's been tracking symptoms he hasn't named, who finally decided the data was sufficient to speak.

"You're distracted. You're reacting to things that aren't there." He leans against the desk, arms crossed, and his gaze travels over my face like he's cataloguing every tell. "Last week you were the sharpest person on this floor. This week you're flinching at shadows."

I don't deny it. I can't. The evidence is written all over me — in the bags under my eyes, in the coffee I can't drink, in the way I jolted in that conference room like someone had touched a live wire to my spine.
He studies me for a long moment. The silence expands between us, filled with the white noise of distant keyboards and the building's respiratory hum.
"I need to know if this is something I should be worried about," he says, "or something I should be reporting."

There it is. The threat, naked and precise. *Reporting* — HR, performance review, a formal paper trail that follows me out the door. But also: *worried*. He gave me both options. He placed them side by side and let me choose.
He's giving me a chance to tell him the truth.

The truth. Right. The truth is that I've been living three different lives with three different versions of him and the walls between those lives are dissolving and soon I won't be able to tell which one I'm in and the app says it cannot be reversed. The truth is that I smelled the ocean in an elevator and tasted wine in bad coffee and saw a sea that doesn't exist in this world stretched across his conference table like a ghost. The truth is that I am losing my mind, and my mind is the only thing I have left.
I can't tell him that.

I can barely hold the truth in my own head — it shifts and bleeds and doubles, timelines stacking like transparencies on an overhead projector, every version of every moment flickering in and out of focus. If I open my mouth and try to explain, what comes out won't be English. It'll be everything all at once, all three Aveys speaking in overlapping voices, and he'll have me committed before the hour's out.
So I do what I always do. I deflect with half-truth.
"My best friend thinks I'm having a breakdown." The words come out steady, which is a miracle. "Maybe she's right."

It's not a lie. Meera absolutely thinks I'm having a breakdown. She told me so three days ago over pad thai, gesturing at me with a spring roll like a prosecutor entering evidence. And maybe she's right — maybe this is what a breakdown looks like when you're a twenty-six-year-old woman with a borrowed reality and a one-way ticket to convergence.
It's just not the whole truth.

Julian's expression shifts. Something softens — the architecture of his face, that careful Vance construction of control and distance, loosens by exactly one degree. His jaw unclenches. His eyes, which have been cataloguing and measuring and calculating, go quiet.
It lasts one second.
Then the mask returns, smooth and featureless as freshly poured concrete.
"Take tomorrow off," he says. "And eat something. You look like you haven't slept in a week."
I haven't. I've been sleeping in fragments, in stolen hours between shifts, and when I do sleep, I dream in timelines that aren't mine.

He pushes off the desk. Reaches past me for the door handle. The door swings open, spilling the office's white noise back in — phones and voices and the mechanical breathing of a building that doesn't know it's full of ghosts.
He pauses.
Doesn't turn around. His back is to me, shoulders squared, the rigid line of his spine visible through his dress shirt. The fluorescent light catches the pale blond of his hair.
"I don't say that to everyone, Chen."
He leaves.
The door clicks shut behind him. That small, final sound again. Bone in socket. Lock engaging.

I stand in the empty office. The glass walls look out onto the floor — desks and monitors and people who exist fully in one timeline, one body, one unbroken life. I am the only person on this floor who is more than one person. I am the only person on this floor whose hands remember blood that isn't from this world.
My phone buzzes in my blazer pocket.
I don't want to look. I look anyway.
**INTEGRITY: 68%**

Three percentage points in the time it took to have one conversation. Three points gone like breath off a window. How many conversations until the number hits zero? How many days until the walls are down completely, until there's no boundary left at all, until I am not Avery A in Timeline A but every Avery in every timeline, screaming in overlapping frequencies?

I slide the phone back into my pocket. I smooth my blazer. I open the door and step back onto the floor and walk toward my desk with my head up and my shoulders back and every muscle in my body holding me together like a person-shaped container cracking at the seams.
And through the window at the end of the corridor — floor-to-ceiling glass, thirty-four floors up, Manhattan skyline blazing under a clear blue sky — I see rain.

Rain on the glass. Rain that isn't there. Rain that is falling six hundred miles away on a tiny café by the sea where a version of me is sitting with a version of him, and the boundary between that moment and this one is thin as a breath, thin as a membrane, thin as the lie I just told Julian Vance's face.
I blink. The rain is gone.
The sky is clear.
But I can still taste the salt.
